title: Push to registry is still slow on second build without any changes
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

As titled

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Run podman in a podman container as described in https://www.redhat.com/sysadmin/podman-inside-container

2. Follow the tutorial to persist the container storage across builds as described https://developers.redhat.com/blog/2019/08/14/best-practices-for-running-buildah-in-a-container

3. Build an image and push it to the registry: Takes about 20s
4. Build and push the same image again: All the steps are cached and the push take about 2s.
5. Kill the container and rerun it.
6. Image is built and all steps are cached as expected but the push takes again about 20s.

**Describe the results you received:**

It looks like podman has to do some heavy IO although the image was already built and pushed before.

**Describe the results you expected:**

Podman should be aware of the fact that the image already exists in the registry. The push should always be fast across builds.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
podman version 4.2.0
```

**Output of `podman info`:**


comments.body:
Use `skopeo v1.9.2` directly is much much faster and consistent across reruns. I replaced `podman push` with

```
skopeo copy source dest
```

It looks like I fooled myself. I copied the image with skopeo from one registry to another because I used the `docker://` schema instead of the local one. But the issue still exists, podman has to repush the image after I restart the container. I think there is a component that doesn't store its state in the container's storage, but I can't find anything in the docs.
I can't reproduce it when I run `podman push` push on the host machine.

I think I found the issue. Podman / buildah doesn't read the `/etc/containers/storage.conf` to use `graphroot` as the container storage. Pushing with debug level `debug` it prints `time="2022-08-22T08:44:02Z" level=debug msg="Using blob info cache at /var/lib/containers/cache/blob-info-cache-v1.boltdb"` which is wrong and not persisted across container restarts.

> I think I found the issue. Podman / buildah doesn't read the `/etc/containers/storage.conf` to use `graphroot` as the container storage. Pushing with debug level `debug` it prints `time="2022-08-22T08:44:02Z" level=debug msg="Using blob info cache at /var/lib/containers/cache/blob-info-cache-v1.boltdb"` which is wrong and not persisted across container restarts.

Thanks for the nice analysis.  I will double check in the code.
@mtrmac, are you on board with moving the blob-info cache under the graph root?

Yeah, that’s a non-obvious gotcha.

I’m not much of a fan of tying this to the c/storage graph root, though:
- Things like `skopeo copy` might not involve c/storage at all.
- The generic copy code depends on the blob info cache implementations and the code that determines the default path, and I fairly strongly _don’t_ want the generic copy code to depend on most of c/storage.

So… I’m hopeful that just blogs and light guidance could be sufficient. And notably, that’s what the linked blog https://www.redhat.com/sysadmin/podman-inside-container _already_ does: it contains things like `VOLUME /var/lib/containers` (not `/var/lib/containers/storage`; this does include `/var/lib/containers/cache`). OTOH the other blog does use both `-v…:/var/lib/containers` and `-v…:/var/lib/containers/storage` (and I didn’t carefully read either of the blogs now so there might be more subtlety to this).

That's a fair point, @mtrmac.

@StarpTech can you share how you're running the containers?  Are you making use of the `/var/lib/containers/cache` volume?

```
# mkdir /var/lib/containers1
# podman run -v ./build:/build:z -v /var/lib/containers1:/var/lib/containers:Z quay.io/buildah/stable\
buildah  -t image1 bud /build
# podman run -v /var/lib/containers1:/var/lib/containers:Z quay.io/buildah/stable buildah  push \ image1 registry.company.com/myuser
# rm -rf /var/lib/containers1
```

Basically, like this

```sh
mkdir -p ./.cache
podman run --privileged -v "./.cache:/var/lib" 
```

The `/var/lib/containers/cache` is created automatically, but as mentioned above, when I change `graphroot` it will not read from the container's config. This can lead to surprises. For example, some container platforms support only one volume mount. If you change the `graphroot` to `/data` everything works except for the blob cache.

I can reproduce it with buildah as well. Is it really podman related?

@StarpTech, I think the crux of the matter is to always have a volume for `/var/lib/containers/{cache}`.  As @mtrmac mentioned above, changing the location of the blob-info cache is undesirable.

That means that if the graph root _inside_ the container points somewhere else, we still need a volume/cache for `/var/lib/containers/cache`.

You probably could do this with 
mkdir -p ./.cache
podman run --security-opt label=disabled -v "./.cache:/var/lib"

For _unprivileged_ users (not when run as root), the cache location follows $XDG_DATA_HOME / $HOME . Changing either of the two is admittedly, also a fairly big hammer with undesirable side effects.

It would be possible, in principle, to expose the location of the blob info cache as a CLI option (the underlying libraries up to c/common/libimage expose it), but that still requires that users would have to know, and care, about this implementation detail in the first place.

---

> @rhatdan What does it fix?

I think the recommendation is to use the default `/var/lib/` paths and mount volumes on top, rather than changing the graph root and using a volume for the non-default location.

If the platform really only supports one volume mount, that might be difficult — but that also seems to be a nonobvious restriction to me. Is it some widely-used environment we need to care about?
